Real Analysis

Review: Limsup/Liminf, p-Series, Iterative Sequences, and Subsequences

Review: Limsup/Liminf, p-Series, Iterative Sequences, and Subsequences

This lecture consolidates several threads from earlier material — limsup/liminf (Lecture 11), the p-series test and Cauchy criterion (Lectures 12–13), monotone and contractive convergence (Lecture 8, 12), and subsequences (Lecture 9) — through a set of worked examples in preparation for the quiz.

Limsup/Liminf: Definitions and Basic Inequalities

We first recall the tail-based characterization of [[Limit Superior|lim sup⁡\limsuplimsup and lim inf⁡\liminfliminf]] from Lecture 11, since the remaining results in this section build directly on it.

Limsup and Liminf via Tails

Let (xn)(x_n)(xn​) be a real sequence and define the tail suprema and infima by sn:=sup⁡m≥nxm,tn:=inf⁡m≥nxm.s_n := \sup_{m\ge n} x_m, \qquad t_n := \inf_{m\ge n} x_m.sn​:=supm≥n​xm​,tn​:=infm≥n​xm​. Then lim⁡‾n→∞xn=lim⁡n→∞sn,lim⁡‾n→∞xn=lim⁡n→∞tn.\overline{\lim}_{n\to\infty} x_n = \lim_{n\to\infty} s_n, \qquad \underline{\lim}_{n\to\infty} x_n = \lim_{n\to\infty} t_n.limn→∞​xn​=limn→∞​sn​,lim​n→∞​xn​=limn→∞​tn​.

The subadditivity of lim sup⁡\limsuplimsup proved in Lecture 11 rested on a pointwise inequality for tail suprema; we restate that inequality here as a standalone lemma, since it is the reusable core of the argument.

Tailwise Subadditivity of Sup

For every fixed n∈Nn \in \mathbb{N}n∈N and real sequences (xn),(yn)(x_n), (y_n)(xn​),(yn​),

sup⁡m≥n(xm+ym)  ≤  sup⁡m≥nxm+sup⁡m≥nym.\sup_{m\ge n}(x_m+y_m) \;\le\; \sup_{m\ge n} x_m + \sup_{m\ge n} y_m.m≥nsup​(xm​+ym​)≤m≥nsup​xm​+m≥nsup​ym​.
Proof Sketch

For all m≥nm \ge nm≥n, xm≤sup⁡k≥nxkx_m \le \sup_{k\ge n} x_kxm​≤supk≥n​xk​ and ym≤sup⁡k≥nyky_m \le \sup_{k\ge n} y_kym​≤supk≥n​yk​; adding these and taking the supremum of the left side over m≥nm \ge nm≥n preserves the inequality.

Passing this pointwise inequality to the limit — using the Tail Sup/Inf Characterization above to identify each limit with a lim sup⁡\limsuplimsup — recovers the subadditivity theorem from Lecture 11.

Subadditivity of Limsup

For real sequences (xn)(x_n)(xn​) and (yn)(y_n)(yn​),

lim sup⁡n→∞(xn+yn)  ≤  lim sup⁡n→∞xn+lim sup⁡n→∞yn.\limsup_{n\to\infty}(x_n+y_n) \;\le\; \limsup_{n\to\infty} x_n + \limsup_{n\to\infty} y_n.n→∞limsup​(xn​+yn​)≤n→∞limsup​xn​+n→∞limsup​yn​.
Proof

Let un:=sup⁡m≥n(xm+ym)u_n := \sup_{m\ge n}(x_m+y_m)un​:=supm≥n​(xm​+ym​), sn:=sup⁡m≥nxms_n := \sup_{m\ge n} x_msn​:=supm≥n​xm​, tn:=sup⁡m≥nymt_n := \sup_{m\ge n} y_mtn​:=supm≥n​ym​. By the lemma, un≤sn+tnu_n \le s_n + t_nun​≤sn​+tn​ for every nnn. Passing to the limit (using the Order Limit Law, Lecture 7, which preserves non-strict inequalities),

lim⁡n→∞un≤lim⁡n→∞sn+lim⁡n→∞tn,\lim_{n\to\infty} u_n \le \lim_{n\to\infty} s_n + \lim_{n\to\infty} t_n,n→∞lim​un​≤n→∞lim​sn​+n→∞lim​tn​,

i.e. lim sup⁡n→∞(xn+yn)≤lim sup⁡n→∞xn+lim sup⁡n→∞yn\limsup_{n\to\infty}(x_n+y_n) \le \limsup_{n\to\infty} x_n + \limsup_{n\to\infty} y_nlimsupn→∞​(xn​+yn​)≤limsupn→∞​xn​+limsupn→∞​yn​.

The basic properties of Lecture 11 recorded that lim inf⁡xn=−lim sup⁡(−xn)\liminf x_n = -\limsup(-x_n)liminfxn​=−limsup(−xn​); we now prove that identity, which also gives a second, equivalent formula for lim inf⁡\liminfliminf purely in terms of infima.

Liminf Identities

For any real sequence (xn)(x_n)(xn​),

lim⁡‾n→∞xn=−lim⁡‾n→∞(−xn)=lim⁡n→∞inf⁡m≥nxm.\underline{\lim}_{n\to\infty} x_n = -\overline{\lim}_{n\to\infty}(-x_n) = \lim_{n\to\infty} \inf_{m\ge n} x_m.lim​n→∞​xn​=−limn→∞​(−xn​)=n→∞lim​m≥ninf​xm​.
Proof

Using the set identity −sup⁡A=inf⁡(−A)-\sup A = \inf(-A)−supA=inf(−A),

lim⁡‾xn=−lim⁡‾(−xn)=−lim⁡n→∞sup⁡m≥n(−xm)=−lim⁡n→∞(−inf⁡m≥nxm)=lim⁡n→∞inf⁡m≥nxm.\underline{\lim} x_n = -\overline{\lim}(-x_n) = -\lim_{n\to\infty}\sup_{m\ge n}(-x_m) = -\lim_{n\to\infty}\Bigl(-\inf_{m\ge n} x_m\Bigr) = \lim_{n\to\infty}\inf_{m\ge n} x_m.lim​xn​=−lim(−xn​)=−n→∞lim​m≥nsup​(−xm​)=−n→∞lim​(−m≥ninf​xm​)=n→∞lim​m≥ninf​xm​.

Convergence of p-Series Partial Sums (Special Case p=2p=2p=2)

Lecture 13 proved the p-series test using an integral tail bound combined with the Cauchy criterion. Here we revisit the monotone-boundedness route in more explicit detail, and specialize it to p=2p=2p=2 — a case whose convergence will later be needed without reference to its exact value.

Convergence of ∑1/np\sum 1/n^p∑1/np for p>1p>1p>1 via Integrals

For p>1p > 1p>1, define

Sn(p):=∑k=1n1kp.S_n(p) := \sum_{k=1}^n \frac{1}{k^p}.Sn​(p):=k=1∑n​kp1​.

Then (Sn(p))(S_n(p))(Sn​(p)) is increasing and bounded above by

Sn(p)≤1+∫1∞dxxp=1+1p−1.S_n(p) \le 1 + \int_1^\infty \frac{dx}{x^p} = 1 + \frac{1}{p-1}.Sn​(p)≤1+∫1∞​xpdx​=1+p−11​.

Hence (Sn(p))(S_n(p))(Sn​(p)) converges.

Proof

Since every term is positive, Sn(p)S_n(p)Sn​(p) is increasing. For k≥2k \ge 2k≥2, the function f(x)=x−pf(x) = x^{-p}f(x)=x−p is decreasing, so on [k−1,k][k-1,k][k−1,k] its minimum value is at x=kx=kx=k:

1kp≤∫k−1kdxxp.\frac{1}{k^p} \le \int_{k-1}^k \frac{dx}{x^p}.kp1​≤∫k−1k​xpdx​.

Summing from k=2k=2k=2 to nnn,

∑k=2n1kp≤∫1ndxxp=[x1−p1−p]1n=1−n1−pp−1≤1p−1.\sum_{k=2}^n \frac{1}{k^p} \le \int_1^n \frac{dx}{x^p} = \left[\frac{x^{1-p}}{1-p}\right]_1^n = \frac{1-n^{1-p}}{p-1} \le \frac{1}{p-1}.k=2∑n​kp1​≤∫1n​xpdx​=[1−px1−p​]1n​=p−11−n1−p​≤p−11​.

Therefore Sn(p)≤1+1p−1S_n(p) \le 1 + \dfrac{1}{p-1}Sn​(p)≤1+p−11​, so (Sn(p))(S_n(p))(Sn​(p)) is increasing and bounded above, hence convergent by the Monotone Convergence Theorem (Lecture 8).

Special Case p=2p=2p=2

For xn=∑k=1n1k2x_n = \sum_{k=1}^n \dfrac{1}{k^2}xn​=∑k=1n​k21​, the bound above gives xn≤1+∫1∞x−2 dx=2x_n \le 1 + \int_1^\infty x^{-2}\,dx = 2xn​≤1+∫1∞​x−2dx=2, so (xn)(x_n)(xn​) converges. (Its limit is ζ(2)=π2/6\zeta(2) = \pi^2/6ζ(2)=π2/6, though this exact value is not needed to establish convergence — only the bound xn≤2x_n \le 2xn​≤2.)

Monotone/Cauchy Criteria for the Iteration xn+1=2+xnx_{n+1} = \sqrt{2+x_n}xn+1​=2+xn​​

This example is structurally identical to the recursive sequence xn+1=2xnx_{n+1} = \sqrt{2x_n}xn+1​=2xn​​ analyzed in Lecture 8, and to the recursive square-root sequence of Lecture 12 — the same two-step recipe (establish monotonicity and boundedness, then solve the limiting equation) applies, and we additionally show the contractive route from Lecture 12 gives an independent proof.

Convergence and Limit of xn+1=2+xnx_{n+1} = \sqrt{2+x_n}xn+1​=2+xn​​

Let x1≥0x_1 \ge 0x1​≥0 and define xn+1=2+xnx_{n+1} = \sqrt{2+x_n}xn+1​=2+xn​​. Then (xn)(x_n)(xn​) converges to 222.

  • If x1≤2x_1 \le 2x1​≤2, then (xn)(x_n)(xn​) is monotone increasing and bounded above by 222.
  • If x1≥2x_1 \ge 2x1​≥2, then (xn)(x_n)(xn​) is monotone decreasing and bounded below by 222.
Proof (Monotone + Bounded)

Monotonicity (case x1≤2x_1 \le 2x1​≤2). If xk≤xk+1x_k \le x_{k+1}xk​≤xk+1​, then 2+xk≤2+xk+12+x_k \le 2+x_{k+1}2+xk​≤2+xk+1​, and since ⋅\sqrt{\cdot}⋅​ is increasing on [0,∞)[0,\infty)[0,∞), xk+1≤xk+2x_{k+1} \le x_{k+2}xk+1​≤xk+2​. Since x1≤2x_1 \le 2x1​≤2 gives x1≤x2x_1 \le x_2x1​≤x2​ as the base case, induction shows (xn)(x_n)(xn​) is increasing.

Boundedness. If xn≤2x_n \le 2xn​≤2, then xn+1=2+xn≤4=2x_{n+1} = \sqrt{2+x_n} \le \sqrt{4} = 2xn+1​=2+xn​​≤4​=2. So xn≤2x_n \le 2xn​≤2 for all nnn; the increasing sequence is bounded above, hence convergent by the Monotone Convergence Theorem.

Identifying the limit. Any limit LLL must satisfy L=2+LL = \sqrt{2+L}L=2+L​ (passing to the limit in the recursion, as in Lecture 8), so L2=L+2L^2 = L+2L2=L+2, i.e.

(L−2)(L+1)=0.(L-2)(L+1) = 0.(L−2)(L+1)=0.

Since xn≥0x_n \ge 0xn​≥0 for all nnn, the limit L≥0L \ge 0L≥0, ruling out L=−1L=-1L=−1. Hence L=2L = 2L=2.

The case x1≥2x_1 \ge 2x1​≥2 is analogous: one checks xn+1≤xnx_{n+1} \le x_nxn+1​≤xn​ and xn≥2x_n \ge 2xn​≥2, so (xn)(x_n)(xn​) decreases to 222.

Alternative: Cauchy (Contraction) Argument

As an alternative to Monotone Convergence, the Conjugate Trick (Lecture 6) combined with the contractive-sequence machinery (Lecture 12) gives a direct Cauchy proof — this is the same computation as the recursive square-root example of Lecture 12, with 2+xn2+x_n2+xn​ in place of xn+2x_n+2xn​+2.

Contraction of Successive Differences

For all nnn,

∣xn+2−xn+1∣=∣2+xn+1−2+xn∣=∣xn+1−xn∣2+xn+1+2+xn.|x_{n+2}-x_{n+1}| = \left|\sqrt{2+x_{n+1}}-\sqrt{2+x_n}\right| = \frac{|x_{n+1}-x_n|}{\sqrt{2+x_{n+1}}+\sqrt{2+x_n}}.∣xn+2​−xn+1​∣=​2+xn+1​​−2+xn​​​=2+xn+1​​+2+xn​​∣xn+1​−xn​∣​.

If xn≥0x_n \ge 0xn​≥0, then 2+xn≥2\sqrt{2+x_n} \ge \sqrt22+xn​​≥2​, so

∣xn+2−xn+1∣≤122 ∣xn+1−xn∣.|x_{n+2}-x_{n+1}| \le \frac{1}{2\sqrt2}\,|x_{n+1}-x_n|.∣xn+2​−xn+1​∣≤22​1​∣xn+1​−xn​∣.

This is the contractive condition with c=122∈(0,1)c = \tfrac{1}{2\sqrt2} \in (0,1)c=22​1​∈(0,1), so (xn)(x_n)(xn​) is Cauchy and hence convergent (Lecture 12). The limit must again solve L=2+LL = \sqrt{2+L}L=2+L​, giving L=2L=2L=2.

A Divergent Monotone Sequence: xn+1=xn+1xnx_{n+1} = x_n + \frac{1}{x_n}xn+1​=xn​+xn​1​

Not every monotone sequence converges — this example uses the Divergence of Monotone Sequences theorem (Lecture 13) in the opposite direction from the previous examples: instead of bounding the sequence, we show it is unbounded and conclude proper divergence.

Monotonicity

If x1=a>0x_1 = a > 0x1​=a>0 and xn+1=xn+1xnx_{n+1} = x_n + \dfrac{1}{x_n}xn+1​=xn​+xn​1​, then xn+1−xn=1xn>0x_{n+1} - x_n = \dfrac{1}{x_n} > 0xn+1​−xn​=xn​1​>0, so (xn)(x_n)(xn​) is strictly increasing.

Divergence to +∞+\infty+∞

The sequence (xn)(x_n)(xn​) is unbounded, and

xn≥a2+2(n−1)⟹xn→+∞.x_n \ge \sqrt{a^2 + 2(n-1)} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad x_n \to +\infty.xn​≥a2+2(n−1)​⟹xn​→+∞.
Proof

Compute the difference of squares:

xn+12−xn2=(xn+1xn)2−xn2=2+1xn2≥2.x_{n+1}^2 - x_n^2 = \left(x_n + \frac{1}{x_n}\right)^2 - x_n^2 = 2 + \frac{1}{x_n^2} \ge 2.xn+12​−xn2​=(xn​+xn​1​)2−xn2​=2+xn2​1​≥2.

Summing this inequality from 111 to n−1n-1n−1 (a telescoping sum),

xn2≥x12+2(n−1)=a2+2(n−1),x_n^2 \ge x_1^2 + 2(n-1) = a^2 + 2(n-1),xn2​≥x12​+2(n−1)=a2+2(n−1),

so xn≥a2+2(n−1)→∞x_n \ge \sqrt{a^2+2(n-1)} \to \inftyxn​≥a2+2(n−1)​→∞ by the Archimedean property. Since (xn)(x_n)(xn​) is increasing and unbounded, the Divergence of Monotone Sequences theorem (Lecture 13) confirms xn→∞x_n \to \inftyxn​→∞.

First Few Terms
x1=a,x2=a+1a,x3=a+1a+1a+1a=a+1a+aa2+1.\begin{aligned} x_1 &= a,\\ x_2 &= a + \frac1a,\\ x_3 &= a + \frac1a + \frac{1}{a+\frac1a} = a + \frac1a + \frac{a}{a^2+1}. \end{aligned}x1​x2​x3​​=a,=a+a1​,=a+a1​+a+a1​1​=a+a1​+a2+1a​.​
Contrast with the previous example

This sequence and xn+1=2+xnx_{n+1}=\sqrt{2+x_n}xn+1​=2+xn​​ both add a positive quantity at each step, yet one converges and the other diverges. The difference is that 1/xn1/x_n1/xn​ does not shrink fast enough to keep the partial sums bounded (indeed xn2x_n^2xn2​ grows linearly), whereas 2+xn\sqrt{2+x_n}2+xn​​ is trapped below the fixed point 222 once x1≤2x_1 \le 2x1​≤2. This is a useful reminder that monotonicity alone never guarantees convergence — boundedness must be checked independently, exactly as the Monotone Convergence Theorem requires.

Subsequence Approaching the Supremum

We close with a result connecting suprema (Lecture 3) back to subsequences (Lecture 9): even when the supremum of a bounded sequence’s terms is never actually attained, some subsequence must still converge to it. This is a concrete instance of the Supremum Characterization from Lecture 4, translated into sequence language.

Subsequence Converging to the Supremum

Let (xn)(x_n)(xn​) be a bounded real sequence and let s=sup⁡{xn:n∈N}s = \sup\{x_n : n\in\mathbb{N}\}s=sup{xn​:n∈N}. If s∉{xn:n∈N}s \notin \{x_n : n\in\mathbb{N}\}s∈/{xn​:n∈N}, then there exists a subsequence (xnk)(x_{n_k})(xnk​​) such that xnk→sx_{n_k} \to sxnk​​→s.

  • The indices can be chosen strictly increasing: n1<n2<⋯n_1 < n_2 < \cdotsn1​<n2​<⋯.
  • The subsequence satisfies s−1k<xnk≤ss - \tfrac1k < x_{n_k} \le ss−k1​<xnk​​≤s for all kkk, hence xnk→sx_{n_k} \to sxnk​​→s.
Proof

For each k∈Nk \in \mathbb{N}k∈N, the number s−1ks - \tfrac1ks−k1​ is not an upper bound of {xn}\{x_n\}{xn​}, since sss is the least upper bound (Supremum Characterization, Lecture 4). So we can pick some index with xnk>s−1kx_{n_k} > s - \tfrac1kxnk​​>s−k1​. Choosing these indices inductively so that nk+1>nkn_{k+1} > n_knk+1​>nk​ (always possible, since infinitely many terms must exceed s−1ks-\tfrac1ks−k1​ — otherwise sss would not be approached) yields a genuine subsequence with

s−1k<xnk≤sfor all k.s - \frac1k < x_{n_k} \le s \qquad \text{for all } k.s−k1​<xnk​​≤sfor all k.

Letting k→∞k \to \inftyk→∞, both bounds converge to sss, so xnk→sx_{n_k} \to sxnk​​→s by the Squeeze Theorem.

If sss is actually attained — say xn0=sx_{n_0} = sxn0​​=s for some n0n_0n0​ — the constant subsequence xnk=sx_{n_k} = sxnk​​=s for all kkk trivially converges to sss as well, so the conclusion holds regardless of whether the supremum is attained.

  • Alternative: Cauchy (Contraction) Argument
  • A Divergent Monotone Sequence: xn+1=xn+1xnx_${n+1} = x_n + \frac${1}${x_n}xn+1​=xn​+xn​1​
